title: Replay protection: epoch-boundary semantics differ (Rust strict per-epoch vs Python/C 24-bit counter window)
description: Rust link_layer.rs:242-256: epoch_diff < 0 -> reject (strict per-epoch window, reset on epoch advance; test old_epoch_rejected locks it in). Python replay.py:97-127 and C replay.c:56-148: single 24-bit counter (epoch<<16)|seqnum with half-space arithmetic - a previous-epoch frame within the window of the high-water mark is ACCEPTED once (Python test_epoch_boundary_reordering_accepted locks the opposite behavior in). At seqnum wrap (0xFFFF->epoch+1), legitimately reordered tail frames of the old epoch are delivered by Python/C and dropped by Rust. The spec contradicts itself (spec 02 acceptance table 'Epoch < LastEpoch -> Reject' vs its own 24-bit-counter data model) - ruling needed, then align. Also note replay-state keying differs: C keys windows by EUI-64, Python/Rust by pubkey - after same-EUI key rotation C keeps the window, Python/Rust reset it (interacts with ow1e.57 first-packet acceptance). Distinct from ow1e.57 (first-packet) and 0li1.74/e220/e60z (tx-side wrap).
